title: Trees
description: Binary trees are among the most tested data structures in interviews. Master the TreeNode interface, DFS traversals, BFS level-order, and depth calculations with proper TypeScript types.

sections.title: TreeNode class and interface
sections.explanation: Most interview problems use a `TreeNode` class with `val`, `left`, and `right`. In TypeScript, define it with typed fields — `left` and `right` are `TreeNode | null` since children may not exist. You can also define a separate interface and implement it. Interviewers expect you to write this from memory.

exercise.prompt: Define a TreeNode class with val, left, and right. Create a tree with root=10, left=5, right=15. Print the root value, left child value, and right child value.

validation.message: Tree should have root=10, left=5, right=15
Use typed TreeNode class with nullable children

exercise.hint: Pass 10, 5, 15 to the TreeNode constructors. Use ! after left/right to assert they're not null.

sections.title: DFS traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder)
sections.explanation: Depth-first traversals visit every node using recursion. Inorder (left, root, right) yields sorted order for BSTs. Preorder (root, left, right) is used for tree serialization. Postorder (left, right, root) computes bottom-up results. TypeScript's `TreeNode | null` parameter type naturally handles the base case — check for null.

exercise.prompt: Implement preorder (root, left, right) and postorder (left, right, root) traversals. Build a tree with root=1, left=2, right=3. Print preorder result then postorder result, each joined by comma.

validation.message: Recurse on left and right children with null checks

exercise.hint: Preorder: push val first, then recurse left, then right. Postorder: recurse left, then right, then push val.

sections.title: BFS level-order traversal
sections.explanation: Level-order traversal processes nodes breadth-first using a queue. Dequeue a node, record its value, and enqueue its children. Group nodes by level by iterating over the current queue length each round. This pattern appears in "right side view", "zigzag", and "minimum depth" problems.

exercise.prompt: Implement BFS level-order traversal. Build a tree with root=1, left=2 (children 4,5), right=3 (children 6,7). Print each level as comma-separated values, one level per line.

validation.message: Use typed queue with shift and push for BFS

exercise.hint: shift() dequeues from the front. push() adds children to the back. Process queue.length nodes per level.

sections.title: Tree depth and height
sections.explanation: Maximum depth (height) of a binary tree is the number of nodes along the longest root-to-leaf path. The recursive approach: depth of a null node is 0; otherwise it's 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). This runs in O(n) and is one of the most common interview warm-ups.

exercise.prompt: Implement maxDepth and minDepth (shortest root-to-leaf path). Build a tree: root=1, left=2 (left child 4), right=3. Print the max depth and min depth.

validation.message: Max depth is 3 (1->2->4), min depth is 2 (1->3)
Use Math.max for maxDepth and Math.min for minDepth

exercise.hint: maxDepth uses Math.max of children. minDepth uses Math.min but must handle one-child nodes specially — a null child doesn't count as a leaf.

sections.title: Path sum and collection
sections.explanation: Path problems ask whether a root-to-leaf path with a target sum exists, or to collect all such paths. Subtract the current node's value from the target as you recurse. At a leaf, check if the remaining target equals the node's value. For collecting paths, pass a path array and push a copy when found.

exercise.prompt: Implement hasPathSum that returns true if any root-to-leaf path sums to the target. Build a tree: root=1, left=2 (children 4,5), right=3. Check path sums 7 (1+2+4) and 10. Print each result.

validation.message: Path 1->2->4=7 exists, no path sums to 10
Subtract current val and recurse on both children

exercise.hint: At a leaf, check if node.val === target (remaining sum). Otherwise recurse with target - node.val on both children.
